concepts.title: Parametric Form
concepts.conceptBody:
Differentiation of a function defined by a parameter

Parametric Equation 

When both $\text{x}$ and $\text{y}$ are functions of a third variable $\text{t}$, then given equation are said to be parametric equation and $\text{t}$ is called the parameter.

Let $\text{x}=\text{f}\left( \text{t} \right)$

and $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ y}=\phi \left( \text{t} \right)$ (i)

be function of variable $t$ and inverse function of $x=f\left( t \right)$ be $t=\psi \left( x \right)$, then

$\text{y}=\phi \left( \text{t} \right)=\phi \left[ \psi \left( \text{x} \right) \right]$ (ii)

will be a function of $\text{x}$.

Equation (i) is the parametric form of equation (ii) and $t$ is called the parameter.

To find $\frac{dy}{dx}$ when parametric equations are given: 

Let $\text{x}=\text{f}\left( \text{t} \right)$ and $\text{y}=\phi \left( \text{t} \right)$, be differentiable functions of parameter $\text{t}$ and $\text{t}=\psi \left( \text{x} \right)$ be the inverse function of function $\text{x}=\text{f}\left( \text{t} \right)$, then $\text{y}=\phi \left[ \psi \left( \text{x} \right) \right]$, is a function of $\text{x}$ and $\text{y}$ is a function of $\text{t}$ and $\text{t}$ is a function of $\text{x}$.

Now $\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{dy}{dt}\cdot \frac{dt}{dx}$ (i)

But $\frac{\text{dt}}{\text{dx}}=\frac{1}{\text{dx}/\text{dt}}$ (ii)

$\therefore $ from (i), $\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{dy}{dt}\cdot \frac{1}{dx/dt}=\frac{dy/dt}{dx/dt}$

Hence $\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{dy/dt}{dx/dt}$

Working Rule: 

(i) If $x$ and $y$ are functions of parameter $t$, then find $\frac{dx}{dt}$ and $\frac{dy}{dt}$ separately.

(ii) Then find $\frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}}$ by the following formula

$\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{dy/dt}{dx/dt}$

 

Q) If $x=a\left( \theta +\text{sin}\theta \right),y=a\left( 1-\text{cos}\theta \right)$, find $\frac{dy}{dx}$. 

Solution: 

$\text{x}=\left( \theta +\text{sin}\theta \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ $\therefore \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}=\mathrm{a}(1+\cos \theta)$ (i)

and $\text{y}=\text{a}\left( 1-\text{cso}\theta \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ $\therefore \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}=\mathrm{a}(0+\sin \theta)=\mathrm{a} \sin \theta$ (ii)

Now, $\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{dy/d\theta }{dx/d\theta }=\frac{a\text{sin}\theta }{a\left( 1+\text{cos}\theta \right)}=\frac{2\text{sin}\frac{\theta }{2}\text{cos}\frac{\theta }{2}}{2\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\frac{\theta }{2}}$
